First, the next permutation function. Let's write a helper function that takes a string and modifies it to the next permutation.

Function next_permutation_str:

void next_permutation_str(string &s) {
    int i = s.length() - 2;
    while (i >= 0 && s[i] >= s[i+1]) {
        i--;
    }
    if (i == -1) return; // no next permutation, but problem says k exists so this won't happen
    int j = s.length() - 1;
    while (s[j] <= s[i]) j--;
    swap(s[i], s[j]);
    reverse(s.begin() + i + 1, s.end());
}

This is the standard next permutation algorithm.

Implement Fenwick Tree:

class FenwickTree {
public:
    vector<int> tree;
    FenwickTree(int size) {
        tree.resize(size + 1, 0);
    }
    void update(int index, int delta) {
        while (index < tree.size()) {
            tree[index] += delta;
            index += index & -index;
        }
    }
    int query(int index) {
        int sum = 0;
        while (index > 0) {
            sum += tree[index];
            index -= index & -index;
        }
        return sum;
    }
};

Then, compute inversion count:

int compute_inversion_count(const vector<int>& positions) {
    int n = positions.size();
    FenwickTree ft(n);
    int inv_count = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        // x is positions[i], which is 0-based. Add 1 to make it 1-based.
        int x = positions[i] + 1;
        // number of elements <= x is ft.query(x)
        int count_less_equal = ft.query(x);
        inv_count += i - count_less_equal;
        ft.update(x, 1);
    }
    return inv_count;
}

Now, the code for the Solution class:

class Solution {
public:
    int getMinSwaps(string num, int k) {
        string target = num;
        // Generate the k-th next permutation
        for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
            next_permutation(target.begin(), target.end());
        }
        
        // Build the target indices map
        map<char, deque<int>> index_map;
        for (int i = 0; i < target.size(); ++i) {
            index_map[target[i]].push_back(i);
        }
        
        // Generate the positions array
        vector<int> positions;
        for (char c : num) {
            positions.push_back(index_map[c].front());
            index_map[c].pop_front();
        }
        
        // Compute inversion count
        return compute_inversion_count(positions);
    }
    
    int compute_inversion_count(vector<int>& positions) {
        int n = positions.size();
        FenwickTree ft(n);
        int inv_count = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            int x = positions[i] + 1; // 1-based index
            int count_less_equal = ft.query(x);
            inv_count += i - count_less_equal;
            ft.update(x, 1);
        }
        return inv_count;
    }
    
    class FenwickTree {
    public:
        vector<int> tree;
        FenwickTree(int size) {
            tree.resize(size + 1, 0);
        }
        void update(int index, int delta) {
            while (index < tree.size()) {
                tree[index] += delta;
                index += index & -index;
            }
        }
        int query(int index) {
            int sum = 0;
            while (index > 0) {
                sum += tree[index];
                index -= index & -index;
            }
            return sum;
        }
    };
};

Function next_permutation_str:

void next_permutation_str(string &s) {
    int i = s.length() - 2;
    while (i >= 0 && s[i] >= s[i+1]) {
        i--;
    }
    if (i == -1) return; // no next permutation, but problem says k exists so this won't happen
    int j = s.length() - 1;
    while (s[j] <= s[i]) j--;
    swap(s[i], s[j]);
    reverse(s.begin() + i + 1, s.end());
}

This is the standard next permutation algorithm.

Implement Fenwick Tree:

class FenwickTree {
public:
    vector<int> tree;
    FenwickTree(int size) {
        tree.resize(size + 1, 0);
    }
    void update(int index, int delta) {
        while (index < tree.size()) {
            tree[index] += delta;
            index += index & -index;
        }
    }
    int query(int index) {
        int sum = 0;
        while (index > 0) {
            sum += tree[index];
            index -= index & -index;
        }
        return sum;
    }
};

Then, compute inversion count:

int compute_inversion_count(const vector<int>& positions) {
    int n = positions.size();
    FenwickTree ft(n);
    int inv_count = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        // x is positions[i], which is 0-based. Add 1 to make it 1-based.
        int x = positions[i] + 1;
        // number of elements <= x is ft.query(x)
        int count_less_equal = ft.query(x);
        inv_count += i - count_less_equal;
        ft.update(x, 1);
    }
    return inv_count;
}

Wait, let me check. For each x in positions, we add 1 to get x+1? Or x is positions[i], which ranges from 0 to n-1. So adding 1 to make it 1-based. For example, if x is 0, the index in Fenwick Tree is 1. So yes.

So, the code for inversion count is as above.

Putting all together.

Now, code:

But in C++, the Fenwick Tree can be implemented as follows.

But for the code, the Fenwick Tree is inside the function.

Now, the code for the Solution class:

class Solution {
public:
    int getMinSwaps(string num, int k) {
        string target = num;
        // Generate the k-th next permutation
        for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
            next_permutation(target.begin(), target.end());
        }
        
        // Build the target indices map
        map<char, deque<int>> index_map;
        for (int i = 0; i < target.size(); ++i) {
            index_map[target[i]].push_back(i);
        }
        
        // Generate the positions array
        vector<int> positions;
        for (char c : num) {
            positions.push_back(index_map[c].front());
            index_map[c].pop_front();
        }
        
        // Compute inversion count
        return compute_inversion_count(positions);
    }
    
    int compute_inversion_count(vector<int>& positions) {
        int n = positions.size();
        FenwickTree ft(n);
        int inv_count = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            int x = positions[i] + 1; // 1-based index
            int count_less_equal = ft.query(x);
            inv_count += i - count_less_equal;
            ft.update(x, 1);
        }
        return inv_count;
    }
    
    class FenwickTree {
    public:
        vector<int> tree;
        FenwickTree(int size) {
            tree.resize(size + 1, 0);
        }
        void update(int index, int delta) {
            while (index < tree.size()) {
                tree[index] += delta;
                index += index & -index;
            }
        }
        int query(int index) {
            int sum = 0;
            while (index > 0) {
                sum += tree[index];
                index -= index & -index;
            }
            return sum;
        }
    };
};

Wait, but in C++, the FenwickTree is a nested class inside compute_inversion_count. But no, in the code above, FenwickTree is a nested class inside Solution. But the compute_inversion_count is a member function of Solution.

Or the size is the maximum index. For example, when x can be up to n (since positions[i] is 0-based, adding 1 gives up to n). So the FenwickTree is initialized with size n (positions.size()), but the tree is of size (size + 1). So the code for the FenwickTree constructor is correct.
